caseInfo.title: The Silent Roommate
caseInfo.synopsis: A computer science student is found dead in his shared apartment near Pune University. The door was locked from inside, a window is shattered, and every roommate has a story. But stories, like code, can have bugs — and one of them has a fatal error.

setting.locale: Shared 2BHK apartment, Shivaji Nagar, near Savitribai Phule Pune University campus
setting.description: A modest second-floor apartment in a student housing colony. Peeling paint on the exterior, a narrow staircase, and thin walls that carry every argument. The building houses mostly students and young professionals. A single CCTV camera covers the entrance gate.

victim.name: Rohan Kapoor
victim.personality: Intelligent but increasingly paranoid and controlling. Once a cheerful and sociable person, he had become suspicious of those around him after discovering financial discrepancies. Known to be possessive in relationships and confrontational when angry.
victim.background: Rohan grew up in Indore in a middle-class family. He was academically gifted and secured admission to Pune University on merit. Over the past six months, he noticed money disappearing from his wallet and UPI account. He had been quietly investigating, growing more isolated and aggressive. He was also blackmailing Prof. Vikram Rao over manipulated grades, using it to fund expenses his family couldn't cover.

caseInfo.causeOfDeath: Blunt force trauma to the right temporal region of the skull, consistent with a single heavy blow from a solid brass object.
caseInfo.discoveryTime: Tuesday, November 12, 2024, approximately 7:45 AM. Discovered by the building watchman after Arjun Mehta reported Rohan was 'not waking up'.

locations.name: Living Room
locations.description: The main shared space of the apartment. A worn brown sofa faces a wall-mounted TV. Bookshelves line the far wall, filled with textbooks and programming manuals. A study desk sits near the window — the same window that is now shattered. The body of Rohan Kapoor lies near the bookshelf, a dark stain spreading beneath his head. A chalk outline has been drawn by the first responders. The room smells faintly of stale food and copper.

interactiveElements.name: Bookshelf
interactiveElements.description: A tall wooden bookshelf. Several books are out of place and one bookend is missing — its partner, a matching brass elephant, sits alone on the third shelf. A gap in the dust outline shows where the missing bookend once stood.

interactiveElements.name: Shattered Window
interactiveElements.description: The window near the study desk is broken. Jagged glass edges remain in the frame. Shards of glass are scattered on the ground below — but which side are they on?

interactiveElements.name: Floor Near Window
interactiveElements.description: Looking carefully at the floor near the window, you notice there are very few glass fragments inside the room. The majority appear to have fallen outward.

interactiveElements.name: Coffee Table
interactiveElements.description: A low coffee table with two plates of half-eaten dal chawal and a crumpled paper napkin. Two glasses of water, one nearly empty.

interactiveElements.name: Study Desk
interactiveElements.description: Rohan's study desk. Textbooks, a laptop (password-locked), and a folded note tucked under the keyboard.

interactiveElements.name: Body and Surroundings
interactiveElements.description: Rohan lies face-down near the bookshelf. His pockets contain a wallet (₹200 cash, college ID, debit card) but no mobile phone. His phone is conspicuously absent.

interactiveElements.name: TV and Entertainment Unit
interactiveElements.description: A 32-inch LED TV on a basic stand. A streaming stick is plugged in. The TV is off. Nothing unusual.

interactiveElements.name: Sofa
interactiveElements.description: A threadbare brown sofa with a couple of cushions. A hoodie is draped over the armrest — it belongs to Arjun. Nothing in the pockets.

locations.ambiance: The morning light cuts through the broken window, casting sharp shadows across the chalk outline. Dust motes drift through the beam. Outside, auto-rickshaw horns blare and a chai vendor calls out. Inside, everything is unnervingly still.

locations.name: Kitchen
locations.description: A narrow galley kitchen with a two-burner gas stove, a small fridge, and open shelves stacked with instant noodle packets and rice bags. A pressure cooker sits on the stove with dried dal residue. The sink has unwashed utensils. A microwave sits on the counter near the fridge.

interactiveElements.name: Microwave
interactiveElements.description: A small microwave oven. Opening it reveals an unexpected find — a mobile phone, powered off, hidden inside.

interactiveElements.name: Dustbin
interactiveElements.description: A plastic dustbin near the counter. Among vegetable peels and tea bags, you spot a Swiggy delivery bag and a receipt.

interactiveElements.name: Rice Container
interactiveElements.description: A large steel container labeled 'Basmati Rice.' Lifting the inner tray reveals a sealed plastic pouch underneath the rice, containing cash.

interactiveElements.name: Fridge
interactiveElements.description: A small single-door fridge. Contains leftover paneer, a water bottle, some eggs, and energy drinks. Nothing suspicious.

interactiveElements.name: Sink
interactiveElements.description: Unwashed plates, two steel glasses, and a pressure cooker lid. Consistent with dinner for two being prepared the previous night.

locations.ambiance: The kitchen smells of stale dal and dishwashing soap. A single tube light flickers intermittently. A cockroach scurries behind the gas cylinder as you enter.

locations.name: Arjun's Bedroom
locations.description: A small, tidy bedroom with a single bed, a wardrobe, and a study table with a laptop. A poster of Sachin Tendulkar hangs on the wall. The bed is neatly made — unusually so for a student. The room feels almost staged in its tidiness.

interactiveElements.name: Laptop
interactiveElements.description: Arjun's laptop is open but on the lock screen. The wallpaper shows Arjun and Kavya Nair at a café — she appears to be more than just a neighbor.

interactiveElements.name: Study Table Drawer
interactiveElements.description: A drawer with pens, chargers, and a small diary. The diary has a coded entry on the last page.

interactiveElements.name: Wardrobe
interactiveElements.description: Neatly arranged clothes. In the back, behind a stack of jeans, you find a folder with printed bank statements and grade manipulation records.

interactiveElements.name: Under the Mattress
interactiveElements.description: Lifting the mattress reveals nothing — but checking the phone found in the kitchen would show UPI transactions from Rohan's account.

locations.ambiance: The room smells of deodorant and freshly laundered clothes. The ceiling fan rotates slowly. Sounds from the street below filter through a closed but thin window.

locations.name: Building Entrance
locations.description: The main entrance to Shivaji Heights, a five-story student housing building. A single CCTV camera is mounted above the gate, pointing outward at the entrance path. A security guard's logbook sits on a small desk beside the gate. The watchman, Bahadur, is a Nepali man in his 50s who has worked here for three years.

interactiveElements.name: CCTV Monitor
interactiveElements.description: A small TV connected to the DVR system. The watchman can pull up footage from the previous evening. The camera records everyone entering and leaving the building.

interactiveElements.name: Security Logbook
interactiveElements.description: A ruled notebook where visitors sign in and out. Entry for Monday Nov 11: 'Ravi, Swiggy delivery, 8:30 PM, Flat 2B.' No other visitor entries for that evening.

interactiveElements.name: Kavya Nair's Flat (Neighboring Door)
interactiveElements.description: Flat 2A, right next to Rohan and Arjun's Flat 2B. Knocking on the door lets you speak with Kavya Nair, who provides a statement about what she heard.

interactiveElements.name: Notice Board
interactiveElements.description: A building notice board with society meeting minutes, a complaint about loud music from Flat 3A, and a plumber's visiting card. Nothing relevant.

locations.ambiance: The entrance area is dimly lit by a single CFL bulb. Paan stains mark the wall near the gate. The watchman's radio plays old Hindi film songs. A stray dog sleeps near the stairs.

suspects.name: Arjun Mehta
suspects.relationToVictim: Roommate and close friend for over two years
suspects.personality: Outwardly calm and likable. Keeps his spaces tidy. Speaks softly and makes eye contact. Beneath the surface, he is calculating and panics when cornered. Uses politeness as a shield.
suspects.background: Arjun comes from a farming family in Nashik facing severe financial problems after crop failures. His father is in debt to local moneylenders. Arjun's scholarship covers tuition but not living expenses. Over the past year, he began secretly siphoning money from Rohan — starting with small amounts from the wallet, escalating to unauthorized UPI transfers from Rohan's phone while he slept. He has been sending the money home. He is secretly dating Kavya Nair and confides in her.
suspects.motive: Rohan discovered the financial theft and confronted Arjun on Monday evening. Arjun, facing exposure, expulsion, and the collapse of his family's lifeline, struck Rohan in a moment of desperate rage.

alibi.stated: I was at the university central library until 10:30 PM working on my thesis. I came home and found Rohan on the floor. I thought he had passed out — he sometimes drank too much. I went to bed. In the morning, when he wouldn't wake up, I called the watchman.
alibi.truth: Arjun never left the apartment that evening. He was present during the Swiggy delivery at 8:30 PM. He and Rohan had dinner together, then Rohan confronted him about the stolen money around 9:00 PM. The argument escalated and Arjun struck Rohan with the brass bookend at approximately 9:15 PM. He then staged the broken window to suggest a break-in, hid his phone in the microwave (it contained UPI transfer evidence), cleaned himself up, and went to bed, planning to 'discover' the body in the morning.
alibi.verificationMethod: CCTV footage from the building entrance shows Arjun never left the building after returning at 6:45 PM. His phone connected to the apartment WiFi continuously from 6:47 PM onward. The library's digital entry system has no record of his ID being swiped that evening.

secretKnowledge: He has been stealing money from Rohan for over eight months via wallet theft and unauthorized UPI transfers.
He knows the UPI PIN because he watched Rohan enter it.
He broke the window from inside to stage a burglary, which is why glass fell outward.
He hid his phone in the microwave because it contains evidence of the UPI transfers.
Kavya knows about their argument because she heard it through the wall, and he begged her to stay quiet.
The confrontation note under Rohan's keyboard was left by Rohan for Arjun — 'We need to talk about the money. I know it's you.'

greeting.response: Officer, thank God you're here. I still can't believe this has happened. Rohan was... he was my best friend. We've been living together for two years. I came home last night from the library and he was just lying there. I thought he'd passed out — he drinks sometimes, you know? I should have checked on him. I should have...
greeting.revealsInfo: Arjun claims he found Rohan unconscious and assumed he was drunk. Establishes the library alibi.

alibi-details.playerPrompt: Tell me exactly where you were last evening, step by step.
alibi-details.response: I left for the university library around 6:30 PM. I had my thesis submission coming up — fluid dynamics simulation, very demanding work. I was there until about 10:30, then walked home. Must have reached by 10:45 or so. The door was locked from inside — I used my key. Found Rohan on the floor near the bookshelf. The window was broken. I assumed someone had broken in and Rohan had passed out from the shock or... I don't know. I was tired, I didn't think clearly. I just went to sleep.
alibi-details.revealsInfo: Claims he left at 6:30 PM and returned at 10:45 PM. Says door was locked from inside. Mentions the broken window. Admits he didn't call for help — suspicious.

alibi-pressure.playerPrompt: Can anyone at the library confirm you were there?
alibi-pressure.response: I was in the quiet study section on the second floor. It's usually empty that late. I didn't really talk to anyone. The librarian might have seen me come in, but honestly, I'm not sure. I was wearing my headphones, head down, working.
alibi-pressure.revealsInfo: No witnesses for the library alibi. Claims a librarian might have seen him but is uncertain.

library-records.playerPrompt: The library uses digital ID scanners. We can check the entry logs.
library-records.response: Oh... yes, they do have that system. But sometimes it malfunctions, you know? I might have — I think I might have tailgated someone in. The turnstile was stuck once before and the guard just waved people through. It's not always reliable.
library-records.revealsInfo: Pre-emptively explains away the lack of a library entry log. Becoming evasive.

confront-cctv.playerPrompt: We have CCTV footage from the building entrance. It shows you entering at 6:45 PM but never leaving after that.
confront-cctv.response: That... that can't be right. The camera must have a blind spot, or maybe it wasn't recording properly. These cheap cameras, they glitch all the time. I definitely went to the library. Maybe I left through the back? No, there is no back exit... I... look, I was definitely at the library. The camera must be wrong.
confront-cctv.revealsInfo: Cannot explain why CCTV doesn't show him leaving. His alibi is crumbling.

press-harder.playerPrompt: The camera works fine. It captured the Swiggy driver entering and leaving. Why does it show everyone else but not you leaving?
press-harder.response: I... I don't know. I swear I went to the library. Maybe I'm confused about the time. Maybe I left earlier and came back and left again? It was a stressful night, my memory is foggy.
press-harder.revealsInfo: Arjun is contradicting himself and becoming increasingly frantic.

why-no-help.playerPrompt: You found your roommate unconscious on the floor with a broken window and your first instinct was to go to sleep?
why-no-help.response: I know it sounds bad. I know. But Rohan, he... he drank sometimes. Cheap whisky from the army canteen. I've found him passed out before. And I was exhausted, I'd been at the library for hours. I thought he'd wake up with a headache. I didn't see the blood — it was dark, I didn't turn on the main light. I just used my phone flashlight to get to my room. I swear I didn't know he was... that he was...
why-no-help.revealsInfo: Explains away the suspicious behavior of going to sleep. Claims it was dark and he used phone flashlight.

door-locked.playerPrompt: If the window was broken by a burglar, why was the door still locked from inside?
door-locked.response: The door has a spring lock — it locks automatically when you pull it shut. If someone broke in through the window and left through the door, it would have locked behind them. That's how these old doors work.
door-locked.revealsInfo: Provides a prepared explanation for the locked door. The explanation is technically plausible but rehearsed.

confront-glass.playerPrompt: Forensics shows the window glass fell outward — outside the building. A break-in would push glass inward. This window was broken from inside.
confront-glass.response: What? No, that... I don't know anything about that. Maybe Rohan broke it? Maybe he threw something at someone outside? I wasn't here, I was at the library. I don't know what happened with the window.
confront-glass.revealsInfo: Cannot explain the glass pattern. Falls back on the library alibi which has already been disproven.

relationship.playerPrompt: How was your relationship with Rohan? Any recent tension?
relationship.response: We were like brothers, officer. Shared everything — food, notes, Netflix password. Sure, we had small fights like any roommates. Who does the dishes, who left the fan on, that kind of thing. But nothing serious. Never. He was a good guy, a bit hot-headed sometimes, but good.
relationship.revealsInfo: Claims a harmonious relationship. Denies any serious conflict.

last-contact.playerPrompt: When did you last speak to Rohan?
last-contact.response: Yesterday afternoon, around 4 PM, before I left for the library... I mean, before I went to study. We chatted about ordering food for dinner. He said he'd order Swiggy. That was the last time.
last-contact.revealsInfo: Claims last contact was 4 PM. If they discussed ordering dinner together, why does Arjun claim he was at the library during dinner?

evening-routine.playerPrompt: Walk me through a typical evening for you both.
evening-routine.response: Usually one of us cooks or we order food. We eat together, watch something on TV, then study in our rooms. Rohan was usually up late coding. I sleep earlier because I have morning lab. Very routine, very boring.
evening-routine.revealsInfo: Establishes that eating dinner together was normal — contradicts the library alibi timeline.

confront-dinner.playerPrompt: We found two dinner plates with food on the coffee table. If you were at the library, who was Rohan eating with?
confront-dinner.response: Two plates? Maybe he had someone over. A friend from class? Or maybe... maybe the person who... who killed him? They could have had dinner together and then something went wrong.
confront-dinner.revealsInfo: Cannot explain the second plate. Suggests the unknown diner might be the killer — ironically true.

confront-money.playerPrompt: We found a note from Rohan saying 'We need to talk about the money. I know it's you.' Was Rohan accusing you of stealing from him?
confront-money.response: Stealing? Me? That's... that's absurd. I don't know what that note is about. Maybe it was about someone else. Maybe he lent money to someone at college. Rohan was always lending money and then getting angry about it. It has nothing to do with me.
confront-money.revealsInfo: Denies knowledge of the note but becomes visibly agitated.

confront-phone.playerPrompt: We found a phone hidden in the microwave. It's registered to you. Why would you hide your phone there?
confront-phone.response: In the microwave? I... I must have left it there by accident. I sometimes put things down and forget. I was heating something and got distracted. That's all. It's not hidden, it's just... misplaced.
confront-phone.revealsInfo: Weak explanation for the hidden phone. A microwave is not a place you 'accidentally' leave a phone.

confront-upi.playerPrompt: Your phone shows UPI transfers from Rohan's bank account to yours — ₹47,000 over eight months. Care to explain?
confront-upi.response: Those were... he asked me to transfer money for him. He'd give me cash and I'd do UPI for him because his phone had issues sometimes. It was a favour. He asked me to do it. You can ask — well, you can't ask him now, but it was consensual. He knew about every transfer.
confront-upi.revealsInfo: Admits to the transfers but claims they were authorized. This contradicts Rohan's confrontation note accusing theft.

confront-cash.playerPrompt: We found ₹15,000 in cash hidden inside a rice container in the kitchen. Why are you hiding money?
confront-cash.response: That's my savings. Students keep cash around. I don't always use a bank — my family sends money through people coming from the village. There's nothing wrong with keeping cash at home.
confront-cash.revealsInfo: Claims the cash is legitimate savings. But combined with UPI evidence and the confrontation note, a pattern of theft emerges.

final-accusation.playerPrompt: Arjun, the CCTV shows you never left. You ate dinner with Rohan. His note accuses you of stealing. Your phone has the UPI evidence and you hid it. The window was broken from inside. You killed Rohan Kapoor.
final-accusation.response: I... [long pause] ... you don't understand. You don't understand what it's like. My father is drowning in debt. The moneylenders were threatening to take our land — three generations of my family's land. Rohan had money. His parents sent him plenty. He wouldn't even notice a few hundred here and there. But he noticed. He found out. He said he was going to report me to the dean, get me expelled. My scholarship, my family — everything would be gone. I begged him not to. I begged. But he wouldn't listen. He kept shouting, calling me a thief, a parasite. He picked up his phone to call the police. I grabbed the bookend. I didn't mean to hit him that hard. It was one swing. Just one. And then... then he was on the floor and there was blood, so much blood, and he wasn't moving. I panicked. I broke the window to make it look like a burglary, hid my phone, and... I'm sorry. I'm so sorry. He was my friend.
final-accusation.revealsInfo: Full confession. Confirms the motive (money theft discovered), the weapon (brass bookend), the staging (window broken from inside), and the cover-up (hidden phone).

suspects.name: Priya Sharma
suspects.personality: Soft-spoken, literary, slightly anxious. She fidgets with her dupatta when nervous. Despite her gentle exterior, she has a sharp mind and a quiet strength. She was growing tired of Rohan's controlling behavior.
suspects.background: Priya is from Nagpur and lives in the university women's hostel. She and Rohan started dating after meeting at a college cultural event. The relationship was initially sweet but Rohan became increasingly possessive over the past four months — monitoring her social media, demanding to know her whereabouts, and sending aggressive texts when she didn't reply immediately. She had been considering breaking up but hadn't found the courage. Her hostel has a strict 9 PM curfew and a biometric attendance system.
suspects.motive: Rohan's threatening and possessive texts could suggest she wanted to escape the relationship permanently. However, she had no history of violence and was planning to simply break up with him.

alibi.stated: I was at the hostel all evening. After dinner in the mess hall at 7:30 PM, I went to my room and video-called my mother in Nagpur. My roommate Sneha was there the whole time. I had no idea anything had happened until the police called me this morning.
alibi.truth: This is entirely true. Priya was at the hostel from 5 PM onward. The hostel biometric system confirms her entry at 5:02 PM and no exit until the next morning. Her mother can confirm the 8:30 PM video call. Roommate Sneha Kulkarni confirms Priya was in the room all evening.
alibi.verificationMethod: Hostel biometric entry/exit logs, mess hall attendance register, phone call records showing 42-minute call to her mother starting at 8:28 PM, roommate testimony.

secretKnowledge: Rohan had become very possessive and sent her threatening texts like 'If I can't have you, no one can.'
She was planning to break up with Rohan this coming weekend.
Rohan mentioned suspecting his roommate of stealing money about a week ago, but she didn't take it seriously at the time.
She knows Arjun has a secret girlfriend but doesn't know it's Kavya.

greeting.response: Is it true? Is Rohan really... [she starts crying] I'm sorry. I got the call this morning. They said he was found dead. I can't... we were supposed to meet this weekend. I was going to... it doesn't matter now. Please, ask me whatever you need. I want to help find who did this.
greeting.revealsInfo: Genuine grief. Mentions they had plans for the weekend.

alibi-details.playerPrompt: Where were you last evening between 8 PM and midnight?
alibi-details.response: I was at the hostel. I had dinner in the mess at 7:30, then went to my room. I called my mother at around 8:30 — we spoke for about 40 minutes, she was telling me about my cousin's wedding preparations. After that, I studied for a bit and went to sleep by 11. My roommate Sneha was there the entire time. The hostel has biometric entry — you can check, I didn't leave after 5 PM.
alibi-details.revealsInfo: Detailed alibi with multiple verifiable touchpoints. Very hard to fake.

relationship.playerPrompt: How would you describe your relationship with Rohan?
relationship.response: It was... complicated. In the beginning, he was wonderful. Thoughtful, funny, ambitious. He helped me with my grad school applications. But over the last few months, he changed. He became suspicious of everything I did. If I was five minutes late replying to a text, he'd call ten times. He'd get angry if I talked to male classmates. I loved who he used to be, but I was... I was starting to feel trapped.
relationship.revealsInfo: Confirms Rohan's possessive behavior. Provides context for his threatening texts without implicating herself.

threats.playerPrompt: We found messages from Rohan on your phone: 'If I can't have you, no one can.' Can you explain these?
threats.response: Yes, those... those were from last week. We had a fight because I went to a movie with my friends and he wasn't invited. He sent those when he was angry. He always apologized later. He said he didn't mean it. I wanted to believe him. I know how it looks, but I swear Rohan would never actually hurt me. He was just... insecure.
threats.revealsInfo: Provides context that the threatening texts were part of a pattern of possessiveness and were followed by apologies. Suggests Rohan was the threatening one, not Priya.

breakup-plans.playerPrompt: Were you planning to break up with him?
breakup-plans.response: Yes. I'd decided. This weekend, when we were supposed to meet, I was going to tell him it was over. I'd even talked to Sneha about it — she was going to wait nearby in case he got upset. I just wanted to do it in person, not over text. He deserved that much.
breakup-plans.revealsInfo: She had a plan to break up safely, suggesting she was afraid of Rohan's reaction but not homicidal.

rohan-behavior.playerPrompt: Did you notice anything unusual about Rohan recently?
rohan-behavior.response: Actually, yes. About a week ago, he called me very agitated. He said money was disappearing from his account. Small amounts at first, but it had added up. He said he knew who was doing it but wouldn't tell me who. He sounded angry, not scared. Like he was going to confront someone. I told him to go to the police, but he said he wanted to handle it himself.
rohan-behavior.revealsInfo: Critical information: Rohan knew someone was stealing from him and planned to confront them. This points toward the financial motive and the confrontation that led to his death.

rohan-money.playerPrompt: Did Rohan mention who he suspected of stealing?
rohan-money.response: He didn't say a name. But he said something like, 'It's someone I trusted. Someone close.' At the time, I wondered if he meant me, actually. He'd become so paranoid. But now I think... it must have been someone else. Someone he lived with, maybe.
rohan-money.revealsInfo: Rohan suspected someone close — narrows it to someone in his daily life. Combined with the living arrangement, this points toward his roommate.

suspects.name: Prof. Vikram Rao
suspects.occupation: Associate Professor, Computer Science Department, Pune University
suspects.personality: Authoritative, meticulous, and conscious of his reputation. Speaks in measured sentences. Dresses formally even on casual days. Underneath his polished exterior, he is deeply anxious about his career and the secrets he keeps.
suspects.background: Prof. Rao has been at Pune University for 15 years and is up for promotion to full professor. He has a reputation as a strict but fair grader. However, he has been secretly manipulating grades for students whose parents make 'donations' to a fund he controls. Rohan discovered this through database access during a course project and had been blackmailing Prof. Rao for the past three months, extracting ₹10,000 per month in exchange for silence.
suspects.motive: Rohan's blackmail was both financially and psychologically devastating. If exposed, Prof. Rao would lose his career, reputation, and pension. He had reason to want Rohan silenced permanently.

alibi.stated: I was at the Faculty Club dinner for Dr. Meera Joshi's retirement celebration. It started at 7 PM and went until almost 11. I was there the entire time. You can ask any of the attendees — the Dean, the HoD, the entire CS department was there.
alibi.truth: This is true. Prof. Rao arrived at 6:50 PM and left at 10:55 PM. Eight colleagues can confirm his continuous presence. He made a brief toast at 8:30 PM that was recorded on the department's social media page. He had no opportunity to commit the murder.
alibi.verificationMethod: Faculty Club event log, testimony from eight colleagues including the Dean, social media post with timestamp showing him giving a toast at 8:30 PM, cab booking receipt showing departure from Faculty Club at 10:58 PM.

secretKnowledge: He has been manipulating grades for money through a fake 'academic excellence fund.'
Rohan was blackmailing him for ₹10,000 per month.
He knows Rohan had evidence of the grade manipulation stored on a hard drive.
He was considering reporting himself to get ahead of the blackmail, but hadn't yet.
He is relieved Rohan is dead but is genuinely not the killer.

greeting.response: Officer, I heard about Rohan this morning. A terrible tragedy. He was one of my students — bright, very bright. A bit headstrong perhaps, but talented. I'm happy to help in any way I can. I assume this is routine?
greeting.revealsInfo: Composed and professional. Treats the interview as routine. No visible grief but also no nervousness.

alibi-details.response: I was at the Faculty Club for Dr. Meera Joshi's retirement dinner. Started at 7, ended around 11. The entire CS department was there. I gave a small toast around 8:30 — someone even posted it on the department Instagram, if you need proof. After that, I took an Ola home. I can show you the booking receipt.
alibi-details.revealsInfo: Airtight alibi with multiple witnesses, social media evidence, and digital receipts.

relationship-academic.playerPrompt: What was your academic relationship with Rohan?
relationship-academic.response: He was enrolled in my Advanced Database Systems course this semester. A strong student — good with SQL, excellent with data analysis. He did a course project that involved auditing the university's grade database. Very thorough work. Perhaps... too thorough.
relationship-academic.revealsInfo: Hints that Rohan's database project uncovered something sensitive. The 'too thorough' comment is telling.

grade-manipulation.playerPrompt: We found records in Rohan's apartment showing grade changes linked to payments. Your name appears repeatedly. Can you explain?
grade-manipulation.response: [removes glasses, polishes them slowly] Those records... I was going to address this myself. Look, I made a terrible mistake. Some parents approached me — their children were struggling. They offered donations to an academic fund. I adjusted some marks — not dramatically, just enough to pass. It was wrong. I know it was wrong. But I've been teaching for fifteen years on a salary that hasn't kept pace with inflation. I have two daughters in college. It's not an excuse, but it's the reality.
grade-manipulation.revealsInfo: Admits to grade manipulation. Provides context that weakens motive slightly — he was already considering self-reporting.

blackmail.response: Yes. He found the discrepancies during his project and confronted me three months ago. He wanted ₹10,000 a month to keep quiet. I paid. Three times. I was ashamed, trapped. But officer, I did not kill that boy. I was in a room with eight colleagues and the Dean of the faculty until nearly 11 PM. I have no blood on my hands — not literally. My sins are academic, not criminal.
blackmail.revealsInfo: Confirms the blackmail relationship but the alibi is ironclad.

suspects.name: Kavya Nair
suspects.personality: Sharp, composed, and self-assured. A natural people-reader. She is protective of those she cares about and will bend the truth to shield them. Speaks with a slight Kerala accent.
suspects.background: Kavya has lived in Flat 2A for one year. She initially befriended both Rohan and Arjun as neighbors but developed a secret romantic relationship with Arjun over the past five months. They keep it secret because Kavya's conservative family would disapprove of a live-in boyfriend situation, and Arjun didn't want Rohan to tease him. She was aware that Arjun was under financial stress but did not know the extent of his theft from Rohan.
suspects.motive: No direct motive to kill Rohan. However, she has a strong motive to protect Arjun and may obstruct the investigation through false or misleading testimony.

alibi.stated: I was in my flat all evening. I was studying for my marketing exam. I had my earphones in, listening to music. I heard some loud sounds around 9-ish but this building is noisy — people slam doors, the plumbing bangs. I didn't think much of it. I didn't hear anything specific like voices.
alibi.truth: She was indeed in her flat but she clearly heard the argument between Rohan and Arjun through the thin wall between Flats 2A and 2B. She recognized both voices. She heard shouting about money, then a loud crash, then silence. She texted Arjun at 9:22 PM: 'What happened? Are you okay?' He came to her flat at 9:40 PM, visibly shaken, and told her Rohan had 'fallen.' She suspected worse but chose not to press. She agreed not to say anything.
alibi.verificationMethod: Her text messages to Arjun timestamped at 9:22 PM. The thin wall between the flats makes it implausible she heard nothing. Neighbors above (Flat 3A) also reported hearing loud voices.

secretKnowledge: She is secretly dating Arjun Mehta.
She heard the full argument between Rohan and Arjun, including shouting about stolen money.
She heard a loud crash/thud at approximately 9:15 PM followed by sudden silence.
Arjun came to her flat at 9:40 PM looking panicked and told her Rohan 'fell and hit his head.'
She texted Arjun 'What happened? Are you okay?' at 9:22 PM — this is on her phone.
She is lying about not recognizing the voices to protect Arjun.

greeting.response: Oh, hi. Yes, the police told me to expect someone. So awful what happened to Rohan. I didn't know him very well — just neighborly hello-bye stuff. Is there something I can help with?
greeting.revealsInfo: Downplays her connection to both Rohan and Arjun. Presents herself as a distant neighbor.

alibi-details.response: Yes, I was studying all evening. Marketing strategy exam tomorrow. I had my earphones in, mostly listening to lo-fi playlists on YouTube. I ordered dinner from Zomato around 8, ate in my room, and went back to studying. Pretty boring evening, honestly.
alibi-details.revealsInfo: Claims she was wearing earphones — pre-emptive explanation for not hearing the argument.

what-heard.playerPrompt: The walls in this building are quite thin. Did you hear anything from the neighboring flat?
what-heard.response: Like I said, I had earphones in. I heard some thuds around 9-ish, but that's normal for this building. The plumbing makes noises, people upstairs drop things. I didn't hear any voices or anything specific. Sorry I can't be more helpful.
what-heard.revealsInfo: Claims she heard thuds but no voices. This contradicts the building's known thin walls and other neighbors hearing loud voices.

press-hearing.playerPrompt: The neighbors in Flat 3A — one floor up — say they heard loud arguing from 2B. You're right next door with just a thin wall between you. You're telling me you heard nothing?
press-hearing.response: I... well, maybe I heard some raised voices but I couldn't make out words. It could have been the TV. Rohan sometimes watched action movies with the volume up. I really wasn't paying attention. I had my exam to focus on.
press-hearing.revealsInfo: Shifts from 'heard nothing' to 'maybe heard raised voices.' She's adjusting her story under pressure.

relationship-arjun.playerPrompt: How well do you know Arjun Mehta?
relationship-arjun.response: Arjun? He's my neighbor. We say hi, sometimes chat in the corridor. He seems like a nice, quiet guy. We're not close or anything.
relationship-arjun.revealsInfo: Downplays the relationship with Arjun significantly.

confront-relationship.playerPrompt: We found a photo of you and Arjun together on his laptop wallpaper. You're clearly more than just neighbors. Are you dating him?
confront-relationship.response: [sighs] Fine. Yes, Arjun and I have been seeing each other for a few months. We kept it quiet because my family would freak out if they knew I was dating someone in the next flat. But that has nothing to do with what happened to Rohan. We're just two people who are together. That's not a crime.
confront-relationship.revealsInfo: Admits the relationship. This establishes her motive to protect Arjun.

confront-text.playerPrompt: Your phone records show you texted Arjun at 9:22 PM: 'What happened? Are you okay?' Why would you send that if you didn't hear anything?
confront-text.response: [long pause] ...Okay. Okay. I heard the argument. I heard them shouting. I heard Rohan's voice and Arjun's voice. Rohan was yelling about money, calling Arjun a thief. Then there was a loud crash and everything went quiet. I was scared. I texted Arjun. He came to my flat about twenty minutes later. He was shaking. He said Rohan fell and hit his head. He asked me not to say anything. I... I love him, officer. I didn't want to get him in trouble. But I didn't see what happened. I only heard it.
confront-text.revealsInfo: Full disclosure of what she heard: the argument about money, the crash, Arjun coming to her flat in a shaken state. This is powerful corroborating testimony.

protect-arjun.playerPrompt: You know Arjun may have killed someone and you covered for him. Do you understand that's obstruction?
protect-arjun.response: I know. I know it looks terrible. But I didn't see him do anything. I only heard an argument and a noise. People argue all the time. I didn't know Rohan was dead until this morning. Arjun told me Rohan fell. I wanted to believe him. Maybe I chose to believe him. But I didn't help him do anything. I just... stayed quiet. And I know that's wrong too.
protect-arjun.revealsInfo: Acknowledges her complicity but distinguishes between active participation and passive concealment. Her testimony places the argument and the 'crash' at ~9:15 PM.

suspects.name: Ravi Kumar
suspects.relationToVictim: No personal relationship. Delivered food to the flat on the evening of the murder.
suspects.personality: Streetwise, wary of police, speaks in rapid Hindi mixed with Marathi. Has a tough exterior but is fundamentally non-violent. Gets agitated when accused of things he didn't do.
suspects.background: Ravi is from a village near Solapur and came to Pune five years ago. He works as a Swiggy delivery driver and also operates a small-time drug dealing operation on the side — mostly selling marijuana and occasionally MDMA to college students. He has a prior arrest for possession (charges dropped). He delivered food to Flat 2B at 8:30 PM and left within five minutes. He has never been inside the flat beyond the doorway.
suspects.motive: None related to the murder. His drug dealing is a red herring — it explains his nervousness around police but has nothing to do with Rohan's death. Rohan was not a customer of his.

alibi.stated: I delivered food to that flat around 8:30. The guy who answered the door — not the dead one, the other one — he took the bag, I left. I had three more deliveries after that. I was nowhere near that building after 8:35 PM. Check the Swiggy app, it tracks everything.
alibi.truth: Ravi is telling the truth about the delivery. He arrived at 8:30 PM, handed the food to Arjun at the door, and left by 8:33 PM. CCTV confirms his entry and exit. He completed three more deliveries that evening, all tracked by the Swiggy app with GPS data. He had no interaction with Rohan and never entered the flat.
alibi.verificationMethod: Swiggy app delivery log with GPS timestamps, building CCTV showing entry at 8:30 PM and exit at 8:33 PM, subsequent delivery confirmations at 8:52 PM, 9:15 PM, and 9:45 PM at different locations across the city.

secretKnowledge: He deals drugs to college students in the area but Rohan was not a customer.
He noticed both Arjun and Rohan were in the flat when he delivered — Arjun answered the door and Rohan was visible sitting on the sofa behind him.
He thought the atmosphere seemed tense — Arjun seemed distracted and didn't tip.
He knows other delivery drivers who've had run-ins with students but nothing violent.

greeting.response: Look, sahab, I already told the constable everything. I delivered the food and left. That's it. I don't know anything about what happened to anyone. I'm just a delivery guy. Can I go? I have deliveries to make.
greeting.revealsInfo: Anxious and wants to leave quickly. His nervousness is about his drug dealing, not the murder.

alibi-details.playerPrompt: Walk me through your evening deliveries last night.
alibi-details.response: I started my shift at 7. First delivery to Kothrud, second to this building — Flat 2B, Shivaji Heights — at 8:30. After that, I went to Model Colony at 8:52, then FC Road around 9:15, and my last one to Karve Nagar at 9:45. All of it is on the app. GPS tracked, time-stamped. I couldn't have been anywhere near this building after 8:33.
alibi-details.revealsInfo: Detailed delivery log with specific times and locations. Easily verifiable through Swiggy's system.

what-saw.playerPrompt: When you delivered to Flat 2B, what did you see?
what-saw.response: The other guy opened the door — not the dead one. The tall one with the neat hair. He took the bag. I could see the other guy — the one who's dead now — sitting on the sofa behind him. He looked tense, like they were in the middle of something. The tall one didn't tip, which is unusual — he usually gives ₹20. He seemed distracted, like he wanted me to leave fast. I handed over the food and left. Whole thing took maybe two-three minutes.
what-saw.revealsInfo: Critical testimony: Both Arjun and Rohan were in the flat at 8:30 PM. This contradicts Arjun's claim that he was at the library. Also notes the tense atmosphere.

drug-dealing.playerPrompt: We know about your other business, Ravi. The one that isn't on the Swiggy app.
drug-dealing.response: [looks around nervously] I don't know what you're talking about, sahab. I'm a delivery driver. That's what I do. If you're going to accuse me of something, show me proof. Otherwise, this is harassment.
drug-dealing.revealsInfo: Denies drug dealing but his reaction confirms awareness of what's being implied.

drug-pressure.playerPrompt: I'm not interested in your side business right now. I'm interested in whether it connects to this murder. Did Rohan buy from you?
drug-pressure.response: No! Absolutely not. I've never sold anything to anyone in that flat. The guy who died — I didn't even know his name until today. I only know the flat number because of the deliveries. My... other thing... has nothing to do with this. I swear on my mother's life. These are two completely separate things. Please, sahab, don't mix them up.
drug-pressure.revealsInfo: Emphatically denies any drug connection to the victim. His fear is about his dealing being exposed, not about the murder.

regular-deliveries.playerPrompt: How often do you deliver to that flat?
regular-deliveries.response: Maybe once or twice a week. They order a lot — biryani mostly, sometimes dal chawal. The tall guy usually opens the door. He's polite, tips sometimes. The other one — the dead one — I've seen him a couple of times. He seemed more intense, you know? Like he was always thinking about something. But I never had a real conversation with either of them. It's just 'here's your food, here's the money, bye.'
regular-deliveries.revealsInfo: Regular deliveries establish familiarity with the flat but no deeper connection. His observations of the roommates align with other character descriptions.

evidence.name: Brass Bookend (Elephant Design)
evidence.description: A heavy brass bookend in the shape of an elephant, approximately 15 cm tall and weighing 1.2 kg. Found on the floor near the victim's body with visible blood and hair on the base. Its matching partner remains on the bookshelf, and a clear dust outline shows where this one was positioned before it was removed.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The bookend weighs 1.2 kg and the blood/hair match the victim. A partial fingerprint on the trunk section is smudged but matches the right thumb pattern of Arjun Mehta with 8 of 12 comparison points — not conclusive on its own but consistent.
evidence.significance: This is the murder weapon. The blood and hair confirm it struck the victim. The partial fingerprint points toward Arjun but is not conclusive without corroboration.

evidence.name: Broken Window
evidence.description: The window near the study desk is shattered. The glass is single-pane, old construction. Jagged shards remain in the frame. The break pattern is roughly circular with radial cracks extending outward. The window latch was in the locked position at the time of breaking.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The fracture pattern and the position of the latch indicate the window was broken while locked from inside. The force was applied from within the room. No tool marks or external impact evidence found on the frame.
evidence.significance: Staged to simulate a break-in. However, the locked latch and outward glass distribution prove it was broken from inside, indicating a cover-up by someone already in the flat.

evidence.name: Glass Fragment Distribution
evidence.description: Glass fragments from the broken window are found scattered on the ground below the window — on the outside of the building. Only a few small shards are found on the floor inside the room. The distribution ratio is approximately 85% outside, 15% inside.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: This distribution is conclusive for an impact originating from inside the room. A genuine break-in would show the inverse pattern — 85%+ of glass inside. The fragments outside are larger, consistent with being propelled outward by force. The few inside fragments are secondary debris from the frame.
evidence.significance: Definitively proves the window was broken from inside the apartment. This destroys the staged burglary narrative and proves whoever broke the window was already inside — i.e., the killer was an occupant, not an intruder.

evidence.name: Building CCTV Footage (Nov 11, 6 PM - midnight)
evidence.description:
Footage from the single entrance camera at Shivaji Heights. Shows the following movements:
- 6:45 PM: Arjun Mehta enters the building.
- 8:30 PM: Ravi Kumar (Swiggy delivery) enters.
- 8:33 PM: Ravi Kumar exits.
- No other entries or exits for Flat 2B residents until the next morning.
Notably, Arjun Mehta is NOT seen leaving the building at any point during the evening.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The camera is positioned to capture all entry/exit through the single building gate. There is no secondary exit. The footage is continuous with no gaps or tampering. Arjun Mehta's claim that he left for the library at 6:30 PM is directly contradicted — the footage shows him entering (not leaving) at 6:45 PM and never leaving afterward.
evidence.significance: This is the key evidence that breaks Arjun's alibi. He claims he was at the library from 6:30 PM to 10:30 PM, but the camera shows him entering the building at 6:45 PM and never leaving. He was in the apartment the entire evening.

evidence.name: Arjun's Mobile Phone (Hidden in Microwave)
evidence.description: A Samsung Galaxy M34 registered to Arjun Mehta, found powered off inside the microwave oven in the kitchen. The phone was deliberately hidden — it was placed face-down at the back of the microwave behind a plate.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The phone contains UPI payment app history showing unauthorized transfers. The WiFi connection log shows continuous connection to the apartment's WiFi network ('RohanArjun_5G') from 6:47 PM onward on November 11 — this means the phone (and presumably Arjun) was in the apartment all evening. The phone was powered off manually at 9:38 PM.
evidence.significance: The deliberate hiding of the phone suggests Arjun wanted to conceal its contents from investigators. The WiFi log independently confirms he was home all evening, corroborating the CCTV evidence.

evidence.name: UPI Transaction History
evidence.description: Extracted from Arjun's phone. Shows 23 UPI transfers from Rohan Kapoor's bank account (HDFC ending 4471) to Arjun's account (SBI ending 8903), totaling ₹47,300 over eight months. Amounts range from ₹500 to ₹5,000. All transfers were initiated from Rohan's phone using his UPI PIN.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The transfer pattern shows escalation — early transfers were ₹500-1,000 (testing the waters), later ones were ₹3,000-5,000 (growing bolder). The transfers were made at irregular intervals, often late at night (12 AM - 3 AM), suggesting Arjun accessed Rohan's phone while he slept. The total amount (₹47,300) is significant for students — roughly three months of living expenses.
evidence.significance: Establishes the financial motive. Arjun was systematically stealing from Rohan. When Rohan discovered this and threatened to report him, it became the trigger for the murder.

evidence.name: Rohan's Confrontation Note
evidence.description: A folded note found tucked under Rohan's keyboard on the study desk. Written in Rohan's handwriting on lined notebook paper: 'We need to talk about the money. I know it's you. I have proof. If you don't return everything by Wednesday, I'm going to the Dean and the police. — R'
evidence.detailedAnalysis: Handwriting analysis confirms this was written by Rohan Kapoor. The ink is from a blue Cello Gripper pen — the same brand found in Rohan's pen holder. The note was likely written earlier on Monday and left for Arjun to find, or kept as a talking point for the confrontation. The Wednesday deadline suggests Rohan gave Arjun a few days — but the confrontation happened sooner.
evidence.significance: Proves Rohan knew about the theft and had identified Arjun as the culprit. The ultimatum ('Dean and police') explains why Arjun felt cornered — exposure would mean expulsion, scholarship loss, and criminal charges.

evidence.name: Hidden Cash in Rice Container
evidence.description: ₹15,000 in mixed denomination notes (mostly ₹500s and ₹200s) found in a sealed plastic pouch hidden beneath the inner tray of a steel rice container in the kitchen.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The cash is in circulated notes with no sequential serial numbers. The hiding spot is deliberate — someone lifted the inner tray of the rice container and placed the pouch underneath. This is inconsistent with normal savings behavior for a student and suggests concealment of illicitly obtained money.
evidence.significance: Physical evidence of money being hidden. Combined with the UPI transfers, it establishes that Arjun had accumulated stolen funds. The concealment method indicates he knew the money was illicit.

evidence.name: Swiggy Delivery Packaging and Receipt
evidence.description: A Swiggy delivery bag and printed receipt found in the kitchen dustbin. The receipt shows: Order #SWG-7842991, 2x Dal Chawal + 1x Paneer Butter Masala, delivered to Flat 2B, Shivaji Heights, at 8:30 PM, Nov 11. Ordered from 'Annapurna Tiffin Service.' Payment: ₹380 via UPI.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The order was placed from Rohan's Swiggy account at 8:05 PM. The order quantity (food for two) confirms that two people were expected to eat dinner in the flat. The delivery was received at 8:30 PM — the driver (Ravi) confirms Arjun answered the door with Rohan visible behind him.
evidence.significance: Corroborates that both Arjun and Rohan were in the flat at 8:30 PM. The food-for-two order and Ravi's testimony directly contradict Arjun's claim that he was at the library.

evidence.name: Two Dinner Plates on Coffee Table
evidence.description: Two steel plates on the coffee table, both with remnants of dal chawal. One plate is roughly half-eaten, the other nearly finished. Two steel glasses of water sit alongside, one nearly empty. A crumpled paper napkin completes the setting.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The half-eaten plate suggests dinner was interrupted. The food matches the Swiggy order (dal chawal). The presence of two plates indicates two people were eating together in the living room — inconsistent with Arjun's claim of being absent.
evidence.significance: Physical proof that two people ate dinner together in the flat. If Arjun was at the library, who was the second diner? This creates a logical trap — either Arjun was present, or an unknown person ate dinner with Rohan and left no other trace.

evidence.name: Rohan's Missing Mobile Phone
evidence.description: Rohan's phone (OnePlus Nord 3) is not on his body, not at the crime scene, and not found anywhere in the apartment despite a thorough search. His wallet and ID are present. The phone has been unreachable since approximately 9:20 PM on November 11.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The absence of the phone is suspicious. A burglar might take a phone, but they would likely also take the wallet and debit card. The selective absence of only the phone suggests it was removed by someone who wanted to eliminate specific digital evidence — perhaps evidence of the UPI theft, confrontational messages, or call logs.
evidence.significance: Rohan's phone likely contained evidence of the UPI theft (transaction notifications), the confrontation note in digital form, and possibly recordings or call logs. Its disappearance suggests the killer deliberately removed it. The killer would need to know what was on the phone — pointing to someone close to Rohan.

evidence.name: Threatening Texts from Rohan to Priya
evidence.description: Text messages recovered from Priya Sharma's phone showing increasingly possessive and threatening messages from Rohan over the past two months. Notable messages include: 'If I can't have you, no one can' (Nov 5), 'I saw you talking to that MBA guy, is he more interesting than me?' (Nov 8), 'Don't ignore me, you know what happens' (Nov 10).
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The messages show a pattern of escalating possessiveness and veiled threats FROM Rohan TO Priya. Contextually, these messages paint Rohan as a controlling partner but do not suggest Priya had violent intentions. Each threatening message was followed within hours by apologetic messages from Rohan. Priya's responses were consistently de-escalatory.
evidence.significance: Red herring. These texts initially suggest Priya might have a motive (escaping a threatening partner), but closer examination reveals Rohan was the aggressor in the relationship, not Priya. Her responses show she was trying to manage his temper, not plot against him. Her alibi is also airtight.

evidence.name: Grade Manipulation Records
evidence.description: A folder found in Arjun's wardrobe containing printed documents: a spreadsheet of grade changes in the CS department (12 students over 3 semesters), corresponding bank deposits into a fund controlled by Prof. Vikram Rao, and a handwritten note saying 'Insurance — VR knows I have copies.'
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The documents are printouts from the university's grade database. They show systematic grade inflation for specific students, each correlated with a 'donation' to the 'Academic Excellence Fund' — a personal account controlled by Prof. Rao. Rohan created these documents and the 'Insurance' note is in his handwriting. The copies were in the apartment, accessible to Arjun.
evidence.significance: Establishes the blackmail relationship between Rohan and Prof. Rao — providing Rao with a motive. However, Rao's alibi (faculty dinner with 8 witnesses) is airtight. This evidence serves as a plausible but ultimately false lead, redirecting suspicion toward the professor before his alibi clears him.

evidence.name: Kavya Nair's Witness Statement
evidence.description: Initial statement from Kavya Nair (Flat 2A, neighbor): 'I was studying all evening with earphones in. I heard some thuds around 9 PM but thought it was the plumbing or someone upstairs. I didn't hear any voices. I don't know what happened next door.'
evidence.detailedAnalysis: Kavya's statement is inconsistent with the building's known thin walls and the testimony of Flat 3A residents who heard loud arguing from one floor up. Her claim of wearing earphones is a pre-emptive defense. Under pressure, she admits to hearing the full argument and reveals she texted Arjun at 9:22 PM asking if he was okay. She is lying to protect Arjun, her secret boyfriend.
evidence.significance: Initially seems like a dead end, but inconsistencies in her statement (thin walls, other neighbors hearing arguing) can be exploited in interrogation. When broken, her testimony becomes powerful corroborating evidence — she heard the argument about money and the fatal crash at ~9:15 PM.

evidence.name: Autopsy Report - Rohan Kapoor
evidence.description: Preliminary autopsy report from KEM Hospital, Pune. Victim: Rohan Kapoor, Male, 22 years. Cause of death: Blunt force trauma to the right temporal region. Single heavy blow. No defensive wounds on hands or arms, suggesting the attack was sudden and unexpected. Stomach contents indicate a meal consumed approximately 30-60 minutes before death. Time of death estimated between 9:00 PM and 10:00 PM, November 11, 2024.
evidence.detailedAnalysis: The lack of defensive wounds is critical — Rohan did not see the blow coming or was not in a position to defend himself. This suggests either he was turned away from the attacker or the attack came during a moment of distraction in what might have been a heated conversation. The stomach contents (dal and rice) match the Swiggy order delivered at 8:30 PM, placing the meal 30-60 minutes before death — consistent with the 9:00-10:00 PM window.
evidence.significance: Establishes the murder window, confirms the weapon type, and the stomach contents timeline corroborates the dinner scenario. The absence of defensive wounds paints a picture of a sudden attack during or right after a confrontation.

forensics.title: Autopsy Report — Rohan Kapoor
forensics.summary: Death caused by single blunt force trauma to the right temple; TOD between 9-10 PM.
findings: Cause of death: Blunt force trauma to the right temporal region of the skull.
Single impact wound, approximately 4.2 cm in diameter, consistent with a heavy, rounded object.
Depressed skull fracture with subdural hemorrhage. Death would have occurred within minutes.
No defensive wounds on the hands, forearms, or any other body part.
No signs of struggle — clothing undisturbed, no skin under fingernails.
Blood alcohol level: 0.00%. Victim was sober at the time of death.
Stomach contents: Partially digested dal, rice, and paneer, consistent with a meal consumed 30-60 minutes prior to death.
Estimated time of death: Between 9:00 PM and 10:00 PM on November 11, 2024.
No other injuries, toxins, or pre-existing conditions found.
forensics.conclusion: The victim was killed by a single, powerful blow to the right temple by a heavy blunt object. The wound characteristics are consistent with a brass bookend recovered from the scene. The lack of defensive wounds indicates the attack was sudden — the victim was likely not facing the attacker at the moment of impact, or was caught off-guard during a confrontation. The attacker was likely right-handed, striking from the victim's left side.

forensics.title: Fingerprint Analysis — Brass Bookend
forensics.summary: Partial fingerprint on murder weapon matches Arjun Mehta with moderate confidence.
findings: A partial fingerprint was recovered from the trunk portion of the brass elephant bookend.
The print matches 8 of 12 standard comparison points with Arjun Mehta's right thumb.
An 8-point match is considered 'moderate confidence' — sufficient for investigative use but below the 12-point threshold for courtroom certainty in Indian forensic standards.
The partial nature of the print is consistent with the bookend being gripped hastily rather than handled carefully.
Rohan Kapoor's fingerprints are also present on the bookend (expected, as he owned it), but these prints are older (more degraded ridge detail) and on the base, not the striking surface area.
No other unidentified prints were found on the weapon.
forensics.conclusion: The partial fingerprint is consistent with Arjun Mehta having recently handled the bookend. While not conclusive by itself, combined with other evidence, it supports the theory that Arjun picked up and swung the bookend. The hasty grip pattern suggests the bookend was seized in a moment of impulse rather than premeditated weapon selection.

forensics.title: Glass Fracture Analysis — Living Room Window
forensics.summary: Window was definitively broken from inside the apartment, not from outside.
findings: The window is single-pane, 4mm float glass in a wooden frame.
Fracture pattern shows radial cracks originating from the inner surface of the glass.
Conchoidal fracture analysis confirms force was applied from inside the room.
85% of glass fragments by weight were recovered outside the building, below the window. 15% remained inside as secondary debris.
Fragment size distribution outside: predominantly large shards (5-12 cm), indicating high-velocity outward projection.
The window latch was in the locked position at the time of breakage — scratch marks on the latch interior confirm it was engaged when the glass broke.
No tool marks on the frame exterior. No evidence of any instrument being used to break the window from outside.
A small indentation on the inner window frame suggests a hard object (possibly a fist or the bookend) was used to strike the glass from inside.
forensics.conclusion: The window was broken from inside the apartment while the latch was locked. This is completely inconsistent with a break-in or external intrusion. The breakage was staged to simulate a burglary, likely by the perpetrator after the murder to create a false narrative for the crime.

forensics.title: Blood Spatter Pattern Analysis — Crime Scene
forensics.summary: Spatter pattern indicates a right-handed assailant striking from the victim's left side.
findings: The primary blood pool is located beneath the victim's head, near the base of the bookshelf.
Cast-off spatter (from the backswing of the weapon) is found in a linear pattern on the ceiling, trending from left to right when facing the bookshelf.
Transfer stains (bloody contact marks) are found on the bookshelf's third shelf, consistent with the weapon being replaced or dropped near the shelf.
The spatter directionality and cast-off pattern are consistent with a single overhead swing from a right-handed assailant standing to the victim's left.
No spatter was found in the kitchen or bedroom — the attack occurred only in the living room.
A small blood smear was found on the edge of the coffee table, consistent with the assailant brushing past it after the attack.
forensics.conclusion: The blood evidence confirms a single-blow attack by a right-handed person. Arjun Mehta is right-handed (confirmed by pen grip observed during witness statement). The attack location, weapon origin (bookshelf), and spatter pattern are all internally consistent with a scenario where Arjun grabbed the bookend from the shelf and struck Rohan during a face-to-face confrontation.

forensics.title: Mobile Phone & WiFi Connection Analysis
forensics.summary: Arjun's phone was connected to home WiFi all evening, contradicting his library alibi.
findings: Arjun Mehta's phone (Samsung Galaxy M34, IMEI: 3567XXXXXXXXXXXX) connected to WiFi network 'RohanArjun_5G' at 6:47 PM on November 11.
The phone maintained continuous WiFi connection until it was manually powered off at 9:38 PM.
No cellular tower handoffs during this period — the phone did not leave the apartment's coverage area.
The Pune University library's WiFi network ('PULib_Secure') shows NO connection from Arjun's device on November 11.
The university library's digital entry system has no record of Arjun's student ID being swiped on November 11.
At 9:22 PM, the phone received a text message from Kavya Nair's number: 'What happened? Are you okay?'
At 9:38 PM, the phone was powered off manually (normal shutdown sequence logged in battery stats).
Rohan Kapoor's phone (OnePlus Nord 3) last connected to 'RohanArjun_5G' at 8:15 PM. It went offline at 9:18 PM — consistent with being powered off or destroyed.
forensics.conclusion: The phone data conclusively places Arjun in the apartment from 6:47 PM onward. He was not at the university library. The phone's deliberate shutdown at 9:38 PM (23 minutes after the estimated time of attack) and subsequent hiding in the microwave indicate a calculated concealment of digital evidence. The text from Kavya at 9:22 PM corroborates that something alarming happened around 9:15 PM.

puzzles.title: CCTV Timeline Reconstruction
puzzles.description:
The building's CCTV footage has been extracted but the timestamps are jumbled due to a DVR glitch. Arrange the following captured events in the correct chronological order to reconstruct the evening's movement at Shivaji Heights.

Events to arrange:
A) Arjun Mehta enters the building carrying a backpack
B) A stray cat triggers the motion sensor near the gate
C) Ravi Kumar (Swiggy driver) enters with a delivery bag
D) Ravi Kumar exits without the delivery bag
E) The watchman Bahadur makes his 10 PM round with a flashlight
F) Morning: The watchman runs inside after hearing Arjun shout for help

events.label: A
events.description: Arjun Mehta enters the building carrying a backpack

events.label: B
events.description: A stray cat triggers the motion sensor near the gate

events.label: C
events.description: Ravi Kumar (Swiggy driver) enters with a delivery bag

events.label: D
events.description: Ravi Kumar exits without the delivery bag

events.label: E
events.description: The watchman Bahadur makes his 10 PM round with a flashlight

events.label: F
events.description: Morning: The watchman runs inside after hearing Arjun shout for help

solution.explanation: Arjun arrived first at 6:45 PM (and crucially, never left). The stray cat triggered the sensor at 7:12 PM. Ravi arrived at 8:30 PM for the Swiggy delivery and left at 8:33 PM. The watchman did his regular round at 10 PM (seeing nothing unusual from outside). The next morning at 7:45 AM, the watchman responded to Arjun's call. The key insight: Arjun enters but never exits — his library alibi is false.

reward.narrative: With the timeline reconstructed, the critical gap becomes apparent — Arjun Mehta entered at 6:45 PM but never left. His library alibi has no CCTV support.

hints: Deliveries typically happen during dinner hours. When does dinner usually get ordered?
The watchman has a fixed 10 PM security round every night.
Think about who should appear leaving the building if they claim they went to the library — but don't.

puzzles.title: Unlock Arjun's Phone
puzzles.description:
Arjun's Samsung Galaxy M34 was found hidden in the microwave, powered off. After powering it on, you need a 4-digit PIN to unlock it. You notice the following clues:

1. A sticky note on Arjun's desk reads: 'Freedom Year'
2. His email password hint (visible on the lock screen) says: 'The year we became free'
3. A small Indian flag sticker is on the back of the phone case

Enter the 4-digit PIN to unlock the phone.

inputData.clue2: Email password hint: 'The year we became free'
inputData.clue3: Small Indian flag (tricolor) sticker on the phone case
inputData.contextHint: All three clues point to a significant year in Indian history.

solution.explanation: All three clues reference Indian independence. 'Freedom Year,' 'The year we became free,' and the Indian flag all point to 1947 — the year India gained independence from British rule. This is a common cultural touchstone that an Indian student would use as a memorable PIN.

reward.narrative: The phone unlocks. You navigate to the UPI payment app and find a history of 23 unauthorized transfers from Rohan's account — totaling ₹47,300 over eight months. This is the stolen money Rohan discovered.

hints: Think about Indian history — what event is associated with 'freedom'?
India's most celebrated national event happened in the 1940s.
August 15, ____ — the year India gained independence.

puzzles.title: Decode the Diary Entry
puzzles.description:
In Arjun's study desk drawer, you find a small diary. The last entry is written in what appears to be a substitution cipher. Arjun studied cryptography last semester.

The coded entry reads:
'KH IRXQG RXW DERXW WKH PRQHB. L QHHG WR IL[ WKLV EHIRUH ZHGQHVGDB.'

A margin note in the diary reads: 'ROT-3 always works.'

Decode the message.

inputData.cipherText: KH IRXQG RXW DERXW WKH PRQHB. L QHHG WR IL[ WKLV EHIRUH ZHGQHVGDB.
inputData.hint: Each letter is shifted forward by 3 positions in the alphabet. To decode, shift each letter backward by 3.

solution.answer: HE FOUND OUT ABOUT THE MONEY. I NEED TO FIX THIS BEFORE WEDNESDAY.
solution.explanation: This is a Caesar cipher with a shift of 3 (ROT-3). Each letter in the cipher text is shifted 3 positions forward from the original. To decode: K→H, H→E, I→F, R→O, X→U, Q→N, G→D, etc. The decoded message reveals Arjun knew Rohan had discovered the theft and was planning to 'fix' the situation before the Wednesday deadline mentioned in Rohan's confrontation note. The word 'fix' takes on a sinister meaning in context.

reward.narrative: The decoded message chills you: 'HE FOUND OUT ABOUT THE MONEY. I NEED TO FIX THIS BEFORE WEDNESDAY.' Combined with Rohan's note threatening to go to the Dean, this diary entry shows Arjun knew the confrontation was coming — and planned to 'fix' it.

hints: ROT-3 means each letter was shifted 3 places forward. Try shifting backward.
Start with single-letter words: 'L' shifted back 3 gives you 'I'.
The first word 'KH' decodes to a common English pronoun.

timeline.event: Rohan and Arjun chat in the apartment about ordering dinner later. Rohan appears agitated but doesn't say why.
timeline.significance: Establishes both were home in the afternoon and dinner was planned for the evening.

timeline.event: Priya Sharma enters the university women's hostel (biometric log). She does not leave until the next morning.
timeline.significance: Establishes Priya's alibi — she was locked in the hostel during the murder window.

timeline.event: Arjun Mehta enters Shivaji Heights building. CCTV captures him carrying a backpack.
timeline.significance: Arjun claims he left for the library at 6:30 PM, but the CCTV shows him entering (not leaving) the building at 6:45 PM. He never leaves afterward.

timeline.event: Arjun's phone connects to the apartment WiFi network 'RohanArjun_5G'.
timeline.significance: Digital confirmation that Arjun (or at least his phone) was in the apartment from 6:47 PM onward.

timeline.event: Prof. Vikram Rao arrives at Faculty Club for Dr. Meera Joshi's retirement dinner.
timeline.source: Faculty Club event log, colleague testimony
timeline.significance: Establishes Prof. Rao's alibi — he was at a public event with numerous witnesses.

timeline.event: Rohan orders food on Swiggy from his account (Order #SWG-7842991: 2x Dal Chawal, 1x Paneer Butter Masala).
timeline.significance: Rohan ordered food for two — confirming he expected to eat dinner with his roommate, who claims to have been at the library.

timeline.event: Priya Sharma begins a video call with her mother in Nagpur. The call lasts 42 minutes.
timeline.significance: Further solidifies Priya's alibi during the critical time window.

timeline.event: Ravi Kumar (Swiggy driver) arrives at Shivaji Heights with the food delivery. CCTV captures entry.
timeline.significance: Ravi arrives and delivers food. He sees both Arjun and Rohan in the flat — Arjun answers the door, Rohan is on the sofa.

timeline.event: Ravi Kumar exits the building. CCTV confirms departure.
timeline.significance: Ravi leaves within 3 minutes, establishing he had no time or opportunity for violence.

timeline.event: Prof. Vikram Rao gives a toast at the retirement dinner. It is recorded and posted on the department Instagram.
timeline.significance: Timestamped proof that Prof. Rao was at the Faculty Club at 8:30 PM.

timeline.event: Rohan and Arjun eat dinner together in the living room (dal chawal and paneer).
timeline.significance: Both eat together. The two plates on the coffee table confirm this.

timeline.event: Rohan confronts Arjun about the stolen money. He shows him the confrontation note and demands repayment by Wednesday or he'll go to the Dean and police.
timeline.source: ev-confrontation-note, Kavya's testimony (heard arguing about money)
timeline.significance: The confrontation — the direct trigger for the murder.

timeline.event: Argument between Rohan and Arjun escalates. Shouting is audible through the walls.
timeline.significance: Multiple neighbors heard the argument. Kavya recognized both voices.

timeline.event: Arjun grabs the brass bookend from the shelf and strikes Rohan on the right temple. Rohan collapses. He dies within minutes from the brain hemorrhage.
timeline.significance: The murder. A single blow during a heated confrontation.

timeline.event: Rohan's phone goes offline (last connection to WiFi at approximately 9:18 PM).
timeline.significance: Arjun likely powered off and disposed of Rohan's phone to hide evidence of UPI theft notifications and messages.

timeline.event: Kavya Nair texts Arjun: 'What happened? Are you okay?'
timeline.significance: Corroborates the timing — Kavya heard the crash at ~9:15 and texted 7 minutes later.

timeline.event: Arjun breaks the living room window from inside to stage a burglary scene.
timeline.significance: Cover-up action. The glass falling outward proves the staging.

timeline.event: Arjun manually powers off his phone and hides it in the microwave.
timeline.significance: Deliberate evidence concealment — the phone contained UPI transfer history.

timeline.event: Arjun goes to Kavya's flat (2A). He is visibly shaken and tells her Rohan 'fell and hit his head.' He asks her not to say anything.
timeline.significance: Arjun's immediate behavior after the murder — seeking comfort and securing silence from his girlfriend.

timeline.event: Watchman Bahadur makes his regular 10 PM security round. Notices nothing unusual from outside.
timeline.significance: The building appeared normal from the outside. Arjun was already in his room by this time.

timeline.event: Prof. Vikram Rao leaves the Faculty Club, takes an Ola cab home.
timeline.source: Faculty Club log, Ola booking receipt
timeline.significance: Prof. Rao's alibi holds — he was at the dinner until nearly 11 PM.

timeline.event: Arjun 'discovers' Rohan's body and calls the watchman, who alerts the police.
timeline.significance: Arjun stages the 'discovery' to appear innocent. He waited until morning to report, having had all night to compose himself.

solution.method: Single blunt force blow to the right temporal region of the skull during a face-to-face confrontation. The attack was impulsive — Arjun grabbed the nearest heavy object (the bookend from the shelf) and struck Rohan when he felt cornered by the threat of exposure.
solution.motive: Rohan discovered that Arjun had been systematically stealing money from him — ₹47,300 over eight months through unauthorized UPI transfers and wallet theft. When Rohan confronted Arjun on the evening of November 11 and threatened to report him to the Dean and police, Arjun faced the total destruction of his life: expulsion, criminal charges, and the collapse of his family's only financial lifeline. In a moment of desperate rage during the escalating argument, he struck Rohan.
solution.narrative:
On the evening of November 11, 2024, Arjun Mehta returned to his shared apartment at 6:45 PM. His roommate Rohan Kapoor had discovered that Arjun had been stealing from him over the past eight months — a total of ₹47,300 in unauthorized UPI transfers and cash theft. Rohan had written a confrontation note demanding full repayment by Wednesday, threatening to go to the Dean and police if Arjun refused.

At 8:30 PM, a Swiggy delivery arrived. Both men ate dinner together in the living room — an attempt at normalcy before the storm. Around 9:00 PM, Rohan initiated the confrontation, producing his evidence of the theft. The argument escalated rapidly, with shouting audible through the thin apartment walls.

At approximately 9:15 PM, as Rohan threatened to call the police, Arjun grabbed the brass elephant bookend from the shelf and struck Rohan on the right temple. The single, powerful blow caused a depressed skull fracture and fatal brain hemorrhage. Rohan collapsed near the bookshelf and died within minutes.

Panicking, Arjun took several steps to cover his tracks: he powered off Rohan's phone and disposed of it (it has not been recovered), broke the living room window from inside to stage a burglary, powered off his own phone (which contained UPI transfer evidence) and hid it in the kitchen microwave, and went to his girlfriend Kavya Nair in the neighboring flat, telling her Rohan had 'fallen.'

Arjun then returned to the apartment, went to bed, and waited until morning to stage the 'discovery' of Rohan's body, calling the watchman at 7:45 AM.

accusationChecklist.requirement: Correctly identify Arjun Mehta as the killer
Identify the brass bookend as the murder weapon
Establish the financial motive (stolen money / UPI transfers)
Disprove Arjun's library alibi using CCTV evidence
Prove the window was broken from inside (staged crime scene)
Explain the hidden phone as evidence concealment
Present Kavya's corroborating testimony about the argument

hints.text: Focus on what people say they did versus what the physical evidence shows. Someone's story doesn't match up with the hard facts.
The CCTV footage at the building entrance is very revealing. Pay close attention to who enters the building and who leaves — or rather, who doesn't leave.
Glass doesn't lie. When a window is broken, the fragments fall in the direction the force travels. If most of the glass is outside, what does that tell you about where the force came from?
Why would someone hide their own phone in a microwave? What could that phone contain that's worth hiding from investigators? Check the financial apps.

Arjun never left the building. He ate dinner with Rohan. Rohan accused him of stealing ₹47,000. The window was broken from inside to fake a burglary. Arjun's phone — hidden in the microwave — contains proof of the theft. He killed Rohan with the brass bookend during their argument about the money.
